title: The phrasebook that taught me to be polite but not understood
summary: Reflect on how the small, repeated phrases you memorise to survive in a new language slowly harden into a self that is courteous, fluent on the surface, and somehow never quite the person you are in your own tongue.
topic: How rehearsed phrases in a second language compound into an altered self
prompt: Write a 600–800-word reflective essay about a phrase, or a small set of phrases, you learned by rote in a second language, and trace how that repetition slowly shaped who you became in it. Ground it in concrete remembered moments, not generalities about 'language and identity'. Argue, against your own first instinct, whether this borrowed politeness is a loss, a gain, or something harder to classify. Use hedging, free indirect thought, and at least one extended metaphor; reach a qualified judgement rather than a tidy moral.
hintStarters: The first sentence I could say without thinking was an apology, and I have wondered since whether the order of acquisition was itself a kind of…
It is tempting to call the borrowed politeness a mask, and harder to admit that after enough years a mask…
What the phrasebook gave me was a self that worked; what it could not give me was…

sampleText:
The first sentence I could say without thinking, in the language of the country I moved to at twenty-three, was an apology. Not a grand one — just the small, oiled phrase you offer when you brush past someone in a doorway, the verbal equivalent of stepping aside. I had learned it from a phrasebook on the flight over, repeated it under my breath in the queue at immigration, and within a month it left my mouth before I had decided to use it. I have wondered since whether the order of acquisition was itself a kind of instruction. Before I could say what I wanted, I could say that I was sorry to want it.

The phrasebook was honest about its purpose. It did not promise to make me eloquent; it promised to keep me from giving offence. So I learned the soft openings — the phrase that means, roughly, may I trouble you; the one that turns a flat request into a small, deferential question; the closing formula that thanks a stranger for a kindness they have not yet performed. These are not lies. They are the grease in the machinery of a society, and for the first year they did exactly what they were meant to do: they let me buy bread, ask directions, and apologise my way out of a hundred misunderstandings without anyone deciding I was rude.

What I did not anticipate was that rehearsing a thing changes the one who rehearses it. The phrases were not stored in some neutral compartment, to be retrieved and then set down. They became muscle memory, and muscle memory does not stay in the muscle. After three or four years, the deference that began as a survival skill had soaked inward. I found myself softening requests in my own language too, hedging where I would once have simply asked, defaulting to a politeness that no longer felt borrowed because I could no longer locate the seam where the borrowing began. The mask, it turned out, was not a thing I took off in the evenings. It had grown a lining of its own face.

It is tempting to file this under loss, and for a long time I did. The version of me that operated in the new language was courteous, fluent on the surface, and somehow thinner — a watercolour of a person who, at home, had been an oil painting. My jokes did not survive translation, so I stopped reaching for them. My opinions arrived pre-softened, wrapped in so much deferential packing that the thing inside sometimes never got unwrapped at all. People found me pleasant. Almost no one, in that language, found me funny, and fewer still found me difficult, which is another way of saying that fewer of them found me.

And yet I distrust the neatness of calling it a loss, because the account leaves out what the borrowed self could do that the original could not. The politeness that sanded down my edges also made me legible to strangers who had no reason to extend me patience. It bought me time — the most generous gift a foreign tongue can give — by signalling, in advance, that I meant no harm. And the habit of hedging, which I had read as self-erasure, turned out, on inspection, to be a discipline I was the better for: it had taught me to notice the person I was speaking to before I spoke. The grain of my personality had not been destroyed. It had been, at most, rotated against the light, so that surfaces I never used to show were the ones now catching it.

So I am left with something harder to classify than either loss or gain. The phrasebook did not give me a smaller self; it gave me an additional one, built by accretion out of ten thousand small repetitions, and the two do not fully overlap. There are things I can only say in the language I was born into, and things — certain kinds of gentleness, a particular patience with being misunderstood — that I learned only by saying, every day for years, that I was sorry to be in the doorway. I would not choose to be only the polite one. But I have stopped pretending he is a stranger wearing my coat. He is the part of me that the long, slow drip of a borrowed courtesy wore into being, one rehearsed apology at a time, and I no longer know how to want him gone.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Extended metaphor sustained across the essay
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Hình ảnh 'a watercolour of a person who... had been an oil painting' rồi sau đó 'rotated against the light, so that surfaces I never used to show were the ones now catching it' kéo dài một ẩn dụ hội hoạ xuyên suốt bài. Việc nuôi một ẩn dụ qua nhiều đoạn — thay vì dùng nhiều ẩn dụ rời rạc — tạo độ gắn kết và chiều sâu, một dấu hiệu của văn C1 chín muồi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The image 'a watercolour of a person who... had been an oil painting', later picked up as 'rotated against the light, so that surfaces I never used to show were the ones now catching it', sustains one painting metaphor across the essay. Carrying a single metaphor through several paragraphs — rather than scattering unrelated ones — builds cohesion and depth, a mark of mature C1 writing.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Hedging to hold a judgement open
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Các cụm như 'I have wondered since whether...', 'It is tempting to file this under loss', 'I distrust the neatness of calling it a loss', và 'at most, rotated against the light' đều là phép rào đón (hedging). Chúng cho phép người viết nêu một nhận định mà không đóng kín nó, giữ cho lập luận ở trạng thái cân nhắc — đúng yêu cầu 'qualified judgement' của đề.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: Phrases such as 'I have wondered since whether...', 'It is tempting to file this under loss', 'I distrust the neatness of calling it a loss', and 'at most, rotated against the light' are all hedging devices. They let the writer advance a claim without sealing it shut, keeping the argument deliberative — exactly the 'qualified judgement' the prompt asks for.

sampleTranslationVi:
Câu đầu tiên tôi nói được mà không cần suy nghĩ, bằng thứ tiếng của đất nước tôi chuyển đến năm hai mươi ba tuổi, là một lời xin lỗi. Không phải một lời trọng đại — chỉ là câu nói nhỏ, trơn tru mà ta thốt ra khi lướt qua ai đó ở khung cửa, thứ tương đương bằng lời với việc nép sang một bên. Tôi học nó từ một cuốn sách hội thoại trên chuyến bay sang, lẩm nhẩm nó trong hàng người chờ ở cửa nhập cảnh, và chỉ trong một tháng nó đã bật ra khỏi miệng tôi trước cả khi tôi quyết định dùng nó. Từ đó tôi đã tự hỏi liệu thứ tự tiếp thu ấy có phải tự nó là một kiểu chỉ dẫn không. Trước khi nói được điều mình muốn, tôi đã nói được rằng tôi xin lỗi vì đã muốn nó.

Cuốn sách hội thoại trung thực về mục đích của nó. Nó không hứa biến tôi thành người hùng biện; nó hứa giữ cho tôi khỏi làm mất lòng ai. Vậy nên tôi học những lời mở đầu mềm mỏng — câu nói đại khái nghĩa là 'cho phép tôi làm phiền'; câu biến một lời yêu cầu thẳng tuột thành một câu hỏi nhỏ nhún nhường; công thức kết thúc cảm ơn một người lạ vì một sự tử tế họ còn chưa làm. Đây không phải lời dối. Chúng là chất bôi trơn trong bộ máy của một xã hội, và suốt năm đầu chúng làm đúng việc chúng được tạo ra để làm: cho tôi mua bánh mì, hỏi đường, và xin lỗi để thoát khỏi cả trăm hiểu lầm mà không ai phán xét tôi là kẻ thô lỗ.

Điều tôi không lường trước là việc tập đi tập lại một điều gì đó làm thay đổi chính người tập nó. Những câu nói ấy không được cất trong một ngăn trung tính nào đó, để lấy ra rồi đặt xuống. Chúng trở thành trí nhớ cơ bắp, và trí nhớ cơ bắp không nằm yên trong cơ bắp. Sau ba bốn năm, sự nhún nhường vốn khởi đầu như một kỹ năng sinh tồn đã thấm ngược vào bên trong. Tôi thấy mình làm dịu các yêu cầu cả trong tiếng mẹ đẻ, rào đón ở những chỗ trước kia tôi sẽ chỉ hỏi thẳng, tự động quay về một phép lịch sự không còn thấy như đi mượn nữa vì tôi không còn tìm ra được đường nối nơi sự vay mượn bắt đầu. Hoá ra cái mặt nạ không phải thứ tôi tháo ra mỗi tối. Nó đã mọc ra một lớp lót làm bằng chính gương mặt của nó.

Thật cám dỗ khi xếp điều này vào mục mất mát, và đã lâu tôi làm vậy. Phiên bản tôi vận hành trong ngôn ngữ mới thì lịch sự, trôi chảy ở bề mặt, và bằng cách nào đó mỏng hơn — một bức màu nước của một con người mà, ở quê nhà, vốn từng là một bức sơn dầu. Những câu đùa của tôi không sống sót qua phiên dịch, nên tôi thôi với tay tới chúng. Các ý kiến của tôi đến nơi đã được làm dịu sẵn, gói trong quá nhiều lớp đệm nhún nhường đến mức thứ bên trong đôi khi chẳng bao giờ được mở ra. Người ta thấy tôi dễ chịu. Gần như không ai, trong ngôn ngữ ấy, thấy tôi hài hước, và còn ít hơn nữa thấy tôi khó tính, mà đó cũng là một cách nói rằng ít người trong số họ tìm thấy con người thật của tôi hơn.

Thế nhưng tôi ngờ vực cái sự gọn ghẽ của việc gọi đó là một mất mát, vì cách lý giải ấy bỏ sót điều mà cái bản ngã đi mượn làm được mà bản ngã gốc không làm được. Phép lịch sự đã mài nhẵn các góc cạnh của tôi cũng khiến tôi dễ được người lạ hiểu, dù họ chẳng có lý do gì để kiên nhẫn với tôi. Nó mua cho tôi thời gian — món quà hào phóng nhất mà một ngôn ngữ ngoại lai có thể trao tặng — bằng cách báo trước rằng tôi không có ác ý. Và thói quen rào đón, điều mà tôi từng đọc thành sự tự xoá mình, hoá ra, khi xét kỹ, lại là một kỷ luật khiến tôi tốt hơn: nó đã dạy tôi để ý đến người mình đang nói chuyện trước khi mở lời. Thớ cá tính của tôi không bị phá huỷ. Nó, cùng lắm, chỉ bị xoay nghiêng đi dưới ánh sáng, để những bề mặt tôi chưa từng phô ra giờ lại là những bề mặt bắt sáng.

Vậy nên tôi còn lại một điều khó phân loại hơn cả mất mát lẫn được lợi. Cuốn sách hội thoại không cho tôi một bản ngã nhỏ hơn; nó cho tôi thêm một bản ngã, được bồi đắp dần từ mười nghìn lần lặp lại nhỏ nhặt, và hai bản ngã ấy không trùng khít hoàn toàn. Có những điều tôi chỉ có thể nói bằng ngôn ngữ mình sinh ra cùng, và những điều — một kiểu dịu dàng nào đó, một sự nhẫn nại đặc biệt với việc bị hiểu lầm — mà tôi chỉ học được bằng cách nói, mỗi ngày suốt nhiều năm, rằng tôi xin lỗi vì đã đứng ở khung cửa. Tôi sẽ không chọn chỉ là con người lịch sự. Nhưng tôi đã thôi giả vờ rằng anh ta là một kẻ lạ mặc áo khoác của tôi. Anh ta là phần của tôi mà dòng nhỏ giọt dài và chậm của một phép lịch sự đi mượn đã mài nặn nên, mỗi lần một lời xin lỗi đã tập sẵn, và tôi không còn biết cách mong cho anh ta biến mất nữa.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central reflective claim?
options: That phrasebooks are an unreliable way to learn a language.
That the writer regrets ever moving abroad.
That the polite phrases learned by rote in a second language slowly compound into an additional self that does not fully overlap with the original.
That apologising too much is always a character flaw.
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing paragraphs state the phrasebook 'gave me an additional one, built by accretion' and that 'the two do not fully overlap'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at what the writer concludes the phrasebook ultimately gave him.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the writer note that his first fluent phrase was an apology?
options: Because apologising is grammatically the easiest sentence.
Because he had genuinely done something wrong on arrival.
Because the phrasebook only contained apologies.
Because the order in which he acquired phrases felt like an instruction — learning to be sorry to want something before learning to ask for it.
mcQuestions.explanation: He wonders 'whether the order of acquisition was itself a kind of instruction' — sorry-to-want before the wanting itself.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'the order of acquisition' suggest to him?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'muscle memory does not stay in the muscle' mean here?
options: That habits learned for one narrow purpose seep beyond it, reshaping behaviour the writer never meant to change.
That physical exercise affects the brain.
That the writer forgot the phrases over time.
That memorisation is bad for the body.
mcQuestions.explanation: The deference 'had soaked inward', spreading even into his mother tongue — the repetition reshaped more than its target.
mcQuestions.hint: Where did the rehearsed politeness end up spreading to?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best demonstrates the complex concession the prompt requires?
options: Learning languages is good for everyone, full stop.
And yet I distrust the neatness of calling it a loss, because the account leaves out what the borrowed self could do that the original could not.
The phrasebook completely ruined my personality.
There are pros and cons to learning a second language.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B grants the loss reading, then complicates the writer's own conclusion — a layered concession, not a flat 'pros and cons'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the turn that argues against the writer's earlier instinct.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the writer finally conclude about the 'polite' self?
options: That it should be discarded as a fake.
That it is the only real version of him now.
That it is neither pure loss nor pure gain — a self worn into being by repetition that he no longer knows how to want gone.
That it never existed and was imagined.
mcQuestions.explanation: The close holds the paradox open: 'I would not choose to be only the polite one' yet 'I no longer know how to want him gone.'
mcQuestions.hint: Does the writer want the polite self destroyed by the end?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best sustains the essay's extended painting metaphor?
options: The new language had many irregular verbs.
I bought a phrasebook at the airport before the flight.
Politeness is important in every culture around the world.
It had been, at most, rotated against the light, so that surfaces I never used to show were the ones now catching it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B continues the watercolour/oil-painting image with light and surfaces; the others abandon the metaphor.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps the painting-and-light imagery going?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the best cohesion to follow 'It is tempting to file this under loss, and for a long time I did.'?
options: The version of me that operated in the new language was courteous, fluent on the surface, and somehow thinner.
Phrasebooks are sold in most airport bookshops.
In today's globalised world, everyone speaks several languages.
Also, the weather in the new country was often grey.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B develops the 'loss' reading the previous sentence set up; the others break the thread or add filler.
mcQuestions.hint: The sentence introduces the 'loss' view — what should logically elaborate it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence matches the essay's hedged, reflective register?
options: Obviously, learning to apologise destroyed who I truly was.
I distrust the neatness of calling it a loss, because the account leaves out what the borrowed self could do.
Whatever, languages are just languages and none of it matters.
Per my linguistic acquisition, deference was hereby internalised.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is measured and self-questioning; A overstates, C is dismissive, D is bureaucratic jargon.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the controlled, self-doubting claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to 'fewer of them found me'?
options: Fewer people could physically locate the writer.
The writer became less popular over time.
Because his real personality stayed hidden behind the polished phrases, fewer people ever encountered the actual person.
People stopped looking for the writer entirely.
mcQuestions.explanation: The line plays on 'found me funny / difficult' to mean fewer reached the real him beneath the courtesy.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the double meaning of 'found me' after 'found me funny'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the rhetorical function of the final clause, 'I no longer know how to want him gone'?
options: It promises the writer will abandon the second language.
It blames the phrasebook for everything that went wrong.
It announces the writer has fully become a native speaker.
It closes on a qualified acceptance — neither embracing nor rejecting the borrowed self, leaving the tension unresolved.
mcQuestions.explanation: The ending refuses a tidy moral, holding the ambivalence open as the prompt asks.
mcQuestions.hint: Does the ending resolve the tension or hold it open?

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the writer call the polite phrases 'the grease in the machinery of a society'?
options: Because they reduce social friction and let strangers interact smoothly, without being lies.
Because they are dirty and unpleasant.
Because they are mechanical and meaningless.
Because grease eventually wears out and must be replaced.
mcQuestions.explanation: He says they are 'not lies' but the lubricant that let him buy bread and ask directions without giving offence.
mcQuestions.hint: What does grease do in a machine, and why mention it isn't a lie?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is grammatical, on-topic, and matches the essay's tone?
options: Basically the polite me is fake and I hate it, lol.
The deference that began as a survival skill had soaked inward.
Henceforth all requests shall be issued in a deferential register.
Everyone knows foreigners are always more polite than locals.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and reflective; A is slang, C is jargon, D is a sweeping generalisation.
mcQuestions.hint: Match the careful, concrete, reflective voice.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'It had grown a lining of its own face' mean?
options: That the mask was made of expensive fabric.
That the writer's face changed shape physically.
That the borrowed politeness was no longer a removable disguise but had fused into the writer's actual self.
That masks need a lining to be comfortable.
mcQuestions.explanation: The image undoes the mask metaphor: what began as a mask grew its own face, becoming real.
mcQuestions.hint: What happens to a 'mask' that grows a face of its own?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best expresses an idea 'compounding over time', a key theme?
options: The phrase was useful only on the first day.
I learned the phrase and then forgot it.
Politeness is either present or absent, with nothing in between.
He is the part of me that the long, slow drip of a borrowed courtesy wore into being, one rehearsed apology at a time.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B captures slow accumulation — 'long, slow drip', 'one apology at a time' — the compounding the theme requires.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option shows tiny repetitions adding up over years?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option preserves the essay's qualified judgement rather than overstating it?
options: I would not choose to be only the polite one, but I have stopped pretending he is a stranger wearing my coat.
Second languages are a total trap that strips away your soul.
Speaking politely is the highest virtue a person can have.
There is nothing to be learned from living abroad at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B holds both sides at once — refusing the polite-only self yet owning it — the hedged stance the prompt wants.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that accepts two truths at the same time.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to 'made me legible to strangers who had no reason to extend me patience'?
options: It taught strangers to read his handwriting.
The politeness made him easy for others to understand and accept, even when they owed him no goodwill.
Strangers became impatient with him because of his accent.
It made him invisible to everyone around him.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Legible' here means easy to read/accept; the courtesy bought him acceptance from people with no obligation to give it.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to be 'legible' to a stranger who owes you nothing?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a small habit you adopted for one narrow reason — a phrase, a gesture, a way of answering email — that quietly spread and changed how you behave more broadly. Looking back, did that drift cost you something you valued, give you something you needed, or both at once? Could you now undo it if you wanted to?
